topic: Two siblings inherit their grandmother's house
raw_output:
[story 1]

The solicitor’s voice, smooth as aged whiskey, laid out the terms. Two houses, one to each of us. I stared at the deed, the parchment feeling like sandpaper against my fingertips. My stomach clenched, a tight, cold knot forming just beneath my ribs. This inheritance, this sudden imposition, felt like a deliberate slight. I watched my brother, Mark, his face already softening with a kind of naive excitement, his eyes shining with undeserved joy. I didn't offer a word, just a slow, deliberate turn away, letting the silence stretch until it became unbearable. Every corner of the drawing-room seemed to radiate judgment, a silent condemnation of my existence.

[story 2]

I traced the peeling floral wallpaper with a fingernail, the texture rough and mocking. The air in the old house hung thick and stagnant, smelling of dust and forgotten lavender. When I looked at the sprawling, decaying structure, all I saw was a monument to a life I never wanted to touch. Mark was already upstairs, whistling some cheerful tune, oblivious to the sheer weight of this responsibility settling on my shoulders. I pushed my hands deep into my pockets, feeling the rigid tension in my jaw. I wanted to shatter something, anything, just to release the pressure building behind my eyes, the deep, burning resentment that had taken root the moment the news broke.

[story 3]

"Well, this is it," I muttered, the words tasting like ash. The sheer volume of antique furniture in the foyer seemed to mock my meager presence. I surveyed the rooms, cataloging every flaw, every faded tapestry, feeling a sharp, acidic burn rise in my throat. Mark was already inspecting the garden, humming tunelessly, entirely unconcerned with the gloom that clung to the manor. I walked toward the library, my steps heavy and measured, each footfall a deliberate act of defiance against the suffocating atmosphere. I wanted to find a way to make this place uncomfortable, to prove that I was the only one truly capable of enduring its decay.
